coredata.dc:title: Modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl van der Waals heterojunctions with CQDs: Accelerated charge transfer and enhanced photocatalytic activity under vis- and NIR-light
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Runren
Wu, Donghai
Lu, Guanghua
Yan, Zhenhua
Liu, Jianchao
coredata.dc:description: Broadening the range of light absorption and inhibiting charge carrier's recombination are vital to improving photocatalytic activity. In this study, a novel full spectrum light-driven carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl van der Waals (VDW) heterostructures were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. It exhibits remarkable degradation efficiency for removing various antibiotics from water (i.e. tetracycline (TC), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and oxytetracycline (OTC) under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light. The enhancement of photocatalytic performance is due to the 2D-2D VDW heterostructures and CQDs not only accelerate the interfacial charge separation and transfer but also provide massive reactive canters to facilitate degradation reaction. Besides, the optical absorption capacity of CQDs modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl VDW heterostructures are obviously enhanced because of the up-conversion photoluminescence (UC-PL) properties of CQDs. Furthermore, the degradation process of TC is assessed by three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEMs) and liquid chromatography-tandem mass (LC-MS/MS) spectrometry analysis. Active species trapping experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) testify that O2 −, OH, and holes play a crucial part in the photocatalytic degradation process. This work affords a new promising approach for CQDs modified 2D/2D VDW heterojunction with enhanced photoactivity in organic pollutant degradation for full spectrum light utilization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: VDW heterostructures
CQDs-BiOCl/ZnIn2S4
Photocatalytic degradation
Antibiotics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/BiOCl composites
Preparation of CQDs modified ZnIn2S4/BiOCl composites
Reusability and possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 51879228 ), the National Science Funds for Creative Research Groups of China (Grant 51421006 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2018B43614 ), the Program for Scientific Research Innovation Team in Colleges and Universities of Tibet Autonomous Region, and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples.
TEM images of bare BiOCl (a), ZnIn2S4 (b), 10% ZIS/BiOCl (c). The TEM (d), HRTEM (e) and HAADF-STEM (f) images of the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites.
XPS spectra of bare BiOCl, ZnIn2S4 and 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites material. Survey spectrum (a), Bi 4f (b), O 1s (c), Cl 2p (d), S 2p (e), In 3d (f), Zn 2p (g) and C 1s (h).

Photocatalytic degradation of TC with the as-prepared samples (a and d); the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics for TC degradation with the as-prepared samples (b and e); the UV–Vis spectral changes with a reaction time of 10% ZIS/BiOCl and 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl samples on degradation of TC (c and f).
The intermediates and pathways of TC during the photoreaction process.

(a) The four reaction cycles and (b) degradation rate of TC with different scavenger over the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites. (c–d) ESR spectra of DMPO-OH and DMPO-O2 − resulting from visible light irradiation.
The possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism of the CQDs-2D-2D ZIS/BiOCl VDW heterostructure for the degradation of antibiotics under visible light and NIR light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl van der Waals heterojunctions with CQDs: Accelerated charge transfer and enhanced photocatalytic activity under vis- and NIR-light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resources Development of Shallow Lakes of Ministry of Education, College of Environment, Hohai University, Nanjing, 210098, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resources Development of Shallow Lakes of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Water Conservancy Project & Civil Engineering College, Tibet Agriculture & Animal Husbandry University, Linzhi, 860000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Water Conservancy Project & Civil Engineering College

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resources Development of Shallow Lakes of Ministry of Education, College of Environment, Hohai University, Nanjing, 210098, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Integrated Regulation and Resources Development of Shallow Lakes of Ministry of Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Broadening the range of light absorption and inhibiting charge carrier's recombination are vital to improving photocatalytic activity. In this study, a novel full spectrum light-driven carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl van der Waals (VDW) heterostructures were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method. It exhibits remarkable degradation efficiency for removing various antibiotics from water (i.e. tetracycline (TC), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and oxytetracycline (OTC) under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light. The enhancement of photocatalytic performance is due to the 2D-2D VDW heterostructures and CQDs not only accelerate the interfacial charge separation and transfer but also provide massive reactive canters to facilitate degradation reaction. Besides, the optical absorption capacity of CQDs modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl VDW heterostructures are obviously enhanced because of the up-conversion photoluminescence (UC-PL) properties of CQDs. Furthermore, the degradation process of TC is assessed by three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEMs) and liquid chromatography-tandem mass (LC-MS/MS) spectrometry analysis. Active species trapping experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) testify that O2 −, OH, and holes play a crucial part in the photocatalytic degradation process. This work affords a new promising approach for CQDs modified 2D/2D VDW heterojunction with enhanced photoactivity in organic pollutant degradation for full spectrum light utilization.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics, as an important part of pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs), has become an integral part of our lives. However, these products attract special attention because of their excessive abuse in human and veterinary drugs (Van Boeckel et al., 2014; Sarmah et al., 2006; Manyi-Loh et al., 2018). Particularly, improperly disposed of antibiotics molecules can accumulate in water and become hard to remove, which could induce a genetic exchange and cause drug resistance in human beings (Rodriguez-Mozaz et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2015). Distressingly, even trace amounts of antibiotics in water are difficult to remove through conventional biochemical treatment (Wojnarowicz et al., 2014; Kaeseberg et al., 2018; Garcia-Morales et al., 2013). Accordingly, it has already become a critical and pressing task of removing antibiotics from water environments.

Recently, photocatalysis technology has been deemed to a promising strategy for the decontamination organics pollutant owing to it can utilize inexhaustible and clean solar energy (Dong et al., 2015; Reddy et al., 2015). At present, many semiconductors have been investigated, such as Ag-based oxides (Dong et al., 2014), phosphide (Tian et al., 2018), Bi-based oxides (Abay et al., 2017; Che et al., 2018a), where two-dimensional (2D) bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) has attracted broad attention because of its unique and excellent electronic property, low toxicity and high stability (Zhao et al., 2013; Jiang et al., 2019). Nevertheless, the potential application of 2D BiOCl photocatalysts was still restricted by the fast recombination of photoinduced charge carriers and the low utilization of solar energy, which result in poor photocatalytic activity (Ye et al., 2012). Consequently, control morphologies (Huang et al., 2018), metal/nonmetal ion doping (Yang et al., 2018) and construct heterojunction (Ma et al., 2018) have been extensively researched to enhanced photocatalytic performance. Especially, numerous studies indicate that construction of 2D-2D van der Waals (VDW) heterostructure is an ideal pathway to overcome the abovementioned disadvantages (Guan et al., 2017; Ran et al., 2018). Compared with the traditional heterostructure, the 2D-2D VDW heterostructure not only possesses strong interactions and the distinct “face to face” get in touch with a large interface area, but also has the optimized electronic structure for enhanced catalytic performance (Novoselov et al., 2016). Therefore, an efficient BiOCl-based 2D-2D VDW heterostructure with highly improved photocatalytic performance may be necessary.

ZnIn2S4, as an important 2D layered ternary chalcogenide, has attracted wide attention because of its narrow band gap, simple preparation and chemical stability (Su et al., 2016; Gao et al., 2016). Unfortunately, the photocatalytic performance of single ZnIn2S4 is frequently limited, mainly ascribed to the low separation efficiency of charge carriers (Chen et al., 2016). So far, several traditional ZnIn2S4-based heterostructures including ZnS/ZnIn2S4 (Hojamberdiev et al., 2018), ZnIn2S4/TiO2 (Mahadik et al., 2015), ZnIn2S4/Bi2WO6 (Jo et al., 2016) and ZnIn2S4/g-C3N4 (Zhang et al., 2016) have been investigated for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity. Although the above ZnIn2S4-based common heterostructures have prominently improved the photocatalytic activity, it may still have been restricted by the recombination of charge carries. Inspired by the previously reported 2D-2D phosphorene/g-C3N4 (Ran et al., 2018), 2D-2D MoS2/ZnO (Wang et al., 2018) and 2D-2D WS2/WSe2 (Kumar et al., 2018) VDW heterostructure with remarkably accelerate migration ability of photogenerated charge carries and improvement of photocatalytic performance, we believe that fabrication of 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl VDW heterostructure may be a good choice for further increasing photocatalytic activity. However, it is a huge challenge to guarantee that the 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl VDW heterostructure simultaneously possesses abundant active sites and wide the range of light absorption.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of carbon materials, has been extensively applied in various fields such as biological sensing and imaging, fluorescence, electrochemical detection and photoelectrochemical catalysis, which was due to their chemical stability, low toxicity and excellent electrical conductivity (Lim et al., 2015; Du and Guo, 2016; Namdari et al., 2017). Especially from a photocatalysis perspective, CQDs not only possess up-conversion photoluminescence (UC-PL) property, which contributes to an extension the absorption range of the light to near-infrared (NIR) light, but also as electron acceptors to further promote charge carrier separation (Wang et al., 2017; Hu, 2016). Accordingly, CQDs modified 2D-2D VWD heterostructure may simultaneously improve charge separation and optical absorption capacity.

In this work, the CQDs modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl VDW heterostructures were first obtained by a hydrothermal process. It exhibits outstanding universal excellent degradation efficiency for the degradation of TC, CIP and OTC under visible and near-infrared (NIR) light. The enhancement of photocatalytic performance is due to the 2D-2D VDW heterostructures and CQDs not only accelerate the interfacial charge separation and transfer but also provide massive reactive centers to facilitate degradation reaction. Besides, the optical absorption capacity of CQDs modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl VDW heterostructures are obviously enhanced because of the UC-PL properties of CQDs. In addition, the degradation intermediate products of TC are assessed by the 3D EEMs and LC-MS/MS analysis.

ZnCl2, thioacetamide, citric acid, ethylene glycol, and NH4Cl were received from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co Ltd (Beijing, China). InCl3·4H2O, Bi (NO3)3·5H2O, ciprofloxacin (CIP) and ethylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin industrial Inc (Shanghai, China). Tetracycline (TC), and oxytetracycline (OTC) were obtained from J&K Scientific (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ZnIn2S4 and ZnIn2S4/BiOCl composites
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnIn2S4 photocatalysts were synthesized with a hydrothermal process (Yang et al., 2017). Typically, 136 mg ZnCl2, 586 mg InCl3·4H2O, and 460 mg thioacetamide were added to 60 mL ultrapure water and stirred for 20 min; Afterwards, the suspension was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 473.15 K and maintained for 24 h. After the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature, the ZnIn2S4 samples were collected by centrifugation and washed with ultrapure water and ethanol three times. Ultimately, the yellow ZnIn2S4 powders were dried at 343.15 K for overnight.

For the preparation of the ZnIn2S4/BiOCl composites, 1 mmol Bi (NO3)3·5H2O and different amounts of ZnIn2S4 were dispersed in 5 mL ethylene glycol by ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min to form a suspension solution. At the same time, 1 mmol NH4Cl was added to 30 mL of ultrapure water stirred for 10 min to make sure that it's all dissolved, and then added into the abovementioned suspension drop by drop. Afterwards, the above-mentioned mixed solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave at 413.15 K and maintained for 12 h. Finally, the solid powder was collected by centrifugation and washed with ultrapure water and alcohol three times, then dried at 333.15 K for overnight. The prepared ZnIn2S4/BiOCl composites with different weight percentages of ZnIn2S4 were denoted as X% ZIS/BiOCl (where X = 5, 10, 15 and 20). In addition, bare BiOCl was prepared by the same synthesis process without the addition of ZnIn2S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs modified ZnIn2S4/BiOCl composites
ce:section.ce:para: First, the CQDs were prepared through a facile one-step hydrothermal method according to the previous literature (Huang et al., 2016). In a typical procedure, 1.05 g citric acid monohydrate and 335 μL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water, which was continuously stirred for 10 min. Then, the above-obtained suspension was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 453.15 K for 5 h. Afterwards, the dark-brown liquid was subjected to dialysis using a semi-permeable membrane to gain CQDs.

Different amounts of CQDs were ultrasonically dispersed into 20 mL of ultrapure water. Then, 10 wt% ZIS/BiOCl solid powders were dissolved in the above solution with continuous stirring for 20 h. Afterwards, the above-mixed solution was dried at 343.15 K for overnight. Finally, the preparations of the CQDs modified ZnIn2S4/BiOCl composites with different mass ratios of CQDs to 10 wt% ZIS/BiOCl were denoted as 0.5 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl, 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl, 3 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl and 5 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl.

The photocatalytic performance of catalysts was assessed by the degradation of antibiotics (TC, CIP, and OTC) under visible and NIR-light. The visible light (λ > 420 nm) was achieved from a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a UV cutoff filter. The NIR-light was obtained by a 150 W infrared lamp (λ < 700 nm). Briefly, 50 mg of the photocatalyst was added into 10 mg/L of TC, CIP or OTC solution (100 mL, pH = 7) and the suspension was vigorously stirred for 1 h to achieve the adsorption/desorption equilibrium in the dark (298 K). At certain time intervals, 4 mL suspension was collected and filtered by a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the particles and the concentrations of TC (357 nm), CIP (277 nm) and OTC (275 nm) were detected by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The photocatalyst characterization, detailed procedures of an active species trapping experiments, ESR, 3D-EEMs and LC-MS/MS analysis are provided in the Supplementary Material.

The phase and crystal structures of the samples were investigated via the XRD technique. For pure BiOCl, Fig. 1 a shows the characteristic peaks that can be ascribed to the tetragonal phase of BiOCl (JCPDS No. 06-0249) (Huang et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the diffraction peaks at 26.1°, 27.8°, 39.8°, and 47.2° can be ascribed to (006), (102), (108) and (112) crystal planes of pristine ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS No. 65-2023) (Zhang et al., 2016). No other characteristic peaks are observed in BiOCl and ZnIn2S4, demonstrating their high purity. In relation to the ZIS/BiOCl composites, the diffraction peak intensity for BiOCl decreases with the increase in ZnIn2S4 content. Obviously, the positions of the peaks remain unchanged for CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites, suggesting that the incorporated CQDs hardly changed the structure of the ZIS/BiOCl composites (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, no characteristic peaks for the CQDs could be detected in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites, which may be ascribed to small amounts, high dispersion and low crystallinity of CQDs in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites (Xie et al., 2017).

The composition and structure of the as-prepared samples were further verified by Raman and FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S1a, ZnIn2S4 nanosheets present the strongest absorption bands at 121 cm−1, which are attributable to the 2D layered structure of ZnIn2S4. Meanwhile, the absorption peaks at 243 cm−1, 294 cm−1, 343 cm−1 and 367 cm−1 are also assigned to the LO1, TO2, LO2 and A1g modes of ZnIn2S4, respectively (Shen et al., 2012). For the bare BiOCl, two characteristic peaks located at 146.6 cm−1 and 195.4 cm−1 are related to the A1g internal BiCl stretching mode and to the Eg internal BiCl stretching mode, respectively (Sun et al., 2018). It is worth noting that the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites display remarkable two parts of characteristic peaks that tally with ZnIn2S4 (121 cm−1) and BiOCl (146.6 cm−1), respectively. However, the D and G bands of the CQDs could not be observed in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites, owing to the less-loaded CQDs. Moreover, Fig. S1b presents the FT-IR spectra of ZnIn2S4, BiOCl and CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites. The FT-IR spectrum of ZnIn2S4 exhibits the peaks at approximately 1385 cm−1 and 3408 cm−1, which correspond to the surface hydroxyl groups (Jiang et al., 2018). Obviously, the characteristic peaks at 2852 cm−1, and 2922 cm−1 could be related to the stretch vibrations of CH from the residual CTAB (Jo et al., 2016). In addition, the FT-IR of the BiOCl nanosheets shows a peak at 512 cm−1, which agrees with the BiO stretching vibration. Significantly, the characteristic absorption peaks of ZnIn2S4 and BiOCl are observed in the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites, demonstrating the presence of the interfacial interaction between ZnIn2S4 and BiOCl (Fang et al., 2016). Nevertheless, the characteristic peaks of CQDs are also not displayed in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites, which may be due to the lower content of CQDs. Therefore, CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites whether successful formation needs to further research.

The morphologies and elemental composition of as-prepared samples were analyzed by SEM and EDX. Fig. S2a exhibits the SEM images of the pristine BiOCl. As shown in the magnified SEM (Fig. S2b), the typical morphologies of the bare BiOCl are nanosheet with a smooth surface, which presents high uniform-sized microspheres with a diameter of approximately 300–400 nm. Interestingly, in Fig. S2c-d, the as-prepared pure ZnIn2S4 clearly exhibits the 2D layered microsphere structure with a daisy-flower-like morphology consists of many 2D nanosheets. More importantly, it can be clearly seen that the SEM image of the ZIS/BiOCl composites shows the BiOCl nanosheets inserted into the layered ZnIn2S4 (Fig. S2e-f), and the morphologies of the ZIS/BiOCl composites are different from those of ZnIn2S4, indicating that the BiOCl is combined with the 2D layered ZnIn2S4 by VDW forces. However, as shown in Fig. S2g-h, after the loading of the CQDs, the morphology of the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites is not changed obviously owing to the similar size and low content of CQDs. In addition, the elemental composition of the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites was investigated by the EDX (Fig. S2j). Notably, the elements of Bi, C, Cl, In, O, S and Zn are detected in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites, indicating the formation of CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites. Above results demonstrate that CQDs, ZnIn2S4, and BiOCl are in closely contacted with VDW forces, which is in favor of inhibiting charge carrier's recombination to improve the photocatalytic performance.

The morphological features of the as-prepared BiOCl, ZnIn2S4 and CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites were also characterized using TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the pure BiOCl exhibits a batch of square-like nanosheets with a diameter of approximately 300–400 nm. Remarkably, the TEM image of a single ZnIn2S4 shows that the 2D layered daisy-flower-like morphology of ZnIn2S4 is composed of many nanosheets (Fig. 2b). Interestingly, as seen in Fig. 2c, the TEM of ZIS/BiOCl composites displays the BiOCl nanosheets inserted into the layered ZnIn2S4, and it is apparent that the 2D layered daisy-flower-like morphology of the ZnIn2S4 have not been damaged. Even more interesting is fact that the intimate interfaces between the ZnIn2S4 and BiOCl are noticed, implying that the ZIS/BiOCl composites may be composed by the interlayer VDW interactions rather than a physical mixture (Yang et al., 2017). In Fig. 2d, the presence of some smaller CQDs on the surface of ZIS/BiOCl composites, which further suggests that the CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites are successful formation. Additionally, the HRTEM image manifests that three different lattice fringes of 0.27 nm, 0.32 nm, and 0.321 nm are observed in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites (Fig. 2e). The marked lattice spaces of 0.27 nm and 0.32 nm were in good agreement with the (110) plane and (102) planes of the tetragonal phase of BiOCl and hexagonal ZnIn2S4, respectively (Huang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2017). Meanwhile, the fringe spacings of 0.321 nm indexes to the crystal lattices of the CQDs were observed. The HRTEM images further confirm the close linkage between BiOCl nanosheets and ZnIn2S4 nanosheets by VDW forces. Besides, In Fig. 2f, we found that the elements of Bi, C, Cl, In, O, S and Zn uniformly distribute in CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites and consistent with EDX's analysis results. Noticeably, these results further illustrate that the CQDs modified the ZIS/BiOCl composites is prepared by the tight VDW forces connection between the 2D BiOCl nanosheet and the 2D layered ZnIn2S4. This structure is in favor of promoting separation of charge carries and the enhancement of the photocatalytic performance.

The elemental composition and surface chemical states of the as-synthesized samples were investigated by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of the as-prepared samples in Fig. 3 a prove that Bi, In, Cl, C, O, S and Zn elements all exist in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites. It is in keeping with the results obtained from EDX and HAADF-STEM, which indicates that the formation of CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites. Fig. 3b shows that two strong peaks of Bi3+ 4f 5/2 and Bi3+ 4f 7/2 were located at 163.9 eV and 158.6 eV, respectively. It is noteworthy that the Bi 4f peak of CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites exhibits a slight shift towards the high binding energy compared with the pure BiOCl, demonstrating the presence of strong interactions between ZnIn2S4 and BiOCl by VDW forces (Jin et al., 2017). The O1s spectrum in Fig. 3c can be fitted by two peaks at 531.6 eV and 529.8 eV, which are assigned to BiO binding energy and surface hydroxyl groups (BiOH), respectively (Fang et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 3d, the binding energy peaks at 197.2 eV and 199.0 eV are attributed to the Cl 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2 orbits of Cl−1, respectively (Li et al., 2018a; Huang et al., 2018). In addition, the S 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2 binding energies in Fig. 3e are located at 161.1 eV and 162.4 eV, respectively. As seen in Fig. 3f, the binding energies of In 3d 5/2 and In 3d 3/2 are situated at 444.3 eV and 451.8 eV, respectively, which could be ascribed to In3+ in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites (Hojamberdiev et al., 2018). Furthermore, Fig. 3g shows that two peaks at 1021.1eV and 1044.2 eV, which should be assigned to Zn 2p 3/2 and Zn 2p 1/2 of Zn2+, respectively (Zhang et al., 2018). Additionally, the peak of the C1s for the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites at 283.6 eV is attributed to the existence of CC bonds (Xie et al., 2017). These results also demonstrate that the CQDs modified CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites are successfully prepared. Significantly, the above results are also validated that possess intense interfacial interaction by VDW forces between ZnIn2S4 and BiOCl, which could effectively promote the separation of the photogenerated carriers and enhance the photocatalytic activity.

The optical characterizations of the different samples were recorded by UV–vis DRS. As shown in Fig. S3a, the pristine 2D BiOCl nanosheets present the obvious light absorption in the UV region, and the absorption band edges of bare 2D layered ZnIn2S4 are approximately 550 nm, which are in good agreement with the values reported previously (Li et al., 2018a; Huang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2017). It is notable that when the BiOCl nanosheets inserted into the layered ZnIn2S4 nanosheets, the absorption edge of ZIS/BiOCl composites takes place red-shift and the optical absorptivity is improved in comparison with pure BiOCl, which means better visible light harvestability of ZIS/BiOCl composites. It also demonstrates that the interface was tightly interacted by VDW forces between BiOCl and ZnIn2S4 to improve light absorption capacity. More importantly, after being loaded with CQDs, the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites show higher absorption intensity of the visible light, which may be due to the exceptional advantages of CQDs such as high optical absorptivity. Besides, as shown in Fig. S3b, the Eg of 2D BiOCl nanosheets and 2D layered ZnIn2S4 calculated using a plot of (ahv)2 versus (hv) were 3.26 and 2.48 eV, respectively.

The specific surface area (SBET) is also a major influence on the enhancement of photocatalytic performance, which is due to the larger SBET providing more active sites (Che et al., 2018b; Hong et al., 2019). There is no denying that the photocatalytic activity is related to the optical absorption capacity, as well as the specific surface areas. Thus, the SBET of the as-synthesized catalysts were investigated by nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm measurement. In Fig. S4, we found that all the catalysts show a typical type IV with a hysteresis loop. Moreover, the SBET of the pure BiOCl, ZnIn2S4, ZIS/BiOCl and CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites were determined to be 19.18 m2/g, 73.15 m2/g, 28.89 m2/g and 19.83 m2/g, respectively. Apparently, the SBET of the 10% ZIS/BiOCl and 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites are lower than that of the single 2D layered ZnIn2S4, indicating that the BiOCl and CQDs are dispersing and cover on the surface of layered ZnIn2S4. It also indirect demonstrates that the interface was tightly interacted by VDW forces between BiOCl and ZnIn2S4. Therefore, it is reasonable to deduce that the SBET may not be the main factor responsible for the improvement of the photocatalytic activity in CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites.

The photocatalytic performance of the catalysts was evaluated by the visible light degradation of spectrum antibiotic (TC, CIP, and OTC). Fig. 4 a shows that the TC hardly breaks down without the addition of photocatalysts under visible light illumination, suggesting that TC is quite stable. Note that the single 2D BiOCl nanosheets and 2D layered ZnIn2S4 demonstrated a poor photocatalytic activity with 8.2% and 68% degradation rates under 2 h irradiation, which is attributed to the poor visible light absorption capacity and rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. As expected, the 10% 2D-2D ZIS/BiOCl composites display the best photocatalytic activity, which reaches to 83.7%. Interestingly, it is found that the photodegradation ratio is increased at the beginning and then is gradually decreased with the increment of ZnIn2S4 from 5% to 20%. Although the ZIS/BiOCl composites led to excellent visible light adsorption and hinder the recombination of charge carries, the excess loading of ZnIn2S4 impede the contact with BiOCl and affect the photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the time-course variation curves of TC can be further described by the kinetic equation (Eq (1))
(1)
ln
(
C
0
/ C )
= k t .

Surprisingly, the 10% ZIS/BiOCl composites show the supreme apparent reaction rate constant k value (0.0140 min−1), which is 21.87 and 1.42 times that of the pure BiOCl nanosheets (0.00064 min−1) and layered ZnIn2S4 (0.00988 min−1), respectively (Fig. 4b). In addition, it is notable that the absorption peak intensity of TC (357 nm) is gradually weakened as the duration of illumination prolonged (Fig. 4c). Foremost, as shown in Fig. 4d, after the CQDs are introduced to the surface of ZIS/BiOCl composites, the photocatalytic performance is remarkably enhanced. In Fig. 4e, compared with the 10% ZIS/BiOCl composites, the TC degradation ratio of the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites could reach 82.3% in merely 60 min of irradiation. Moreover, the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites material exhibits the highest k (0.0272 min−1) value, which is 1.94 times higher than that of the 10% ZIS/BiOCl (0.0140 min−1). Fig. 4f indicates that the absorbance peak intensity of TC gradually decreased with the increase of light irradiation time. In addition, detail removal rate and reaction rate constant of TC over the photocatalysts are list in Table. S1. These results imply that the 2D-2D VDW heterostructures and CQDs act as effective collectors of the electron are simultaneously improving the separation efficiency of charge carriers, widen absorption capacity of light, and then enhanced the photocatalytic activity.

To further demonstrate the universality of the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites, the CIP and OTC were also chosen as the target pollutants to assess the photocatalytic performance. From Fig. S5, the photocatalytic activities of the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites material degradation for CIP and OTC are higher than the 10% ZIS/BiOCl composites under 90 min. Hence, the verdicts illustrate that the prepared CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites have universality of the degradation for broad-spectrum antibiotics in wastewater.

To further explore the optical property of the CQDs, the UC-PL spectra of the CQDs under different excitation wavelengths were studied (Fig. S6a). As expected, the emissions peaks are located at the visible light region, which means the layered ZnIn2S4 can realize the indirect utilization of the NIR by using the fluorescence effect of the CQDs, then enhancement of the optical absorption response range. To confirm the up-conversion effect of the CQDs on the photocatalytic activity, the 10% ZIS/BiOCl and 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites are investigated for photodegrading of TC under NIR light irradiation. Obviously, the degradation efficiency of the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites material can reach up to 17.7% after 180 min, which is higher than the 10% ZIS/BiOCl composites material under NIR light (Fig. S6b). These results reveal that the UC-PL effect of CQDs can play important roles for the ameliorated NIR photocatalytic performance. In addition, under full spectrum light irradiation 1 wt% -CQDs-10% 2D-2D ZIS/BiOCl shows great improvement of photodegradation, TC can be 95% degraded under 60 min (Fig. S6c). The total organic carbons removal ratio of the different photocatalyst was shown in Fig. S6d, which reveals the degree of the mineralization of TC. The 1 wt% -CQDs-10% 2D-2D ZIS/BiOCl displays the highest mineralization activity (61%), which is 14.5 times, 2.18 times and 1.33 times higher than those of pure BiOCl (4.2%), ZnIn2S4 (28%) and ZIS/BiOCl (46%) in 60 min, respectively. The above results indicate that 1 wt% -CQDs-10% 2D-2D ZIS/BiOCl has sufficient ability to mineralize the TC.

For a deeper investigation of the mineralization property of TC, 3D EEMs technology was adopted. Four samples are collected under different conditions, and the results are shown in Fig. S7. Before irradiation, no obvious fluorescence signal could be observed in the TC solution (Fig. S7a), which is due to the TC molecules having a fluorescence quenching effect (Deng et al., 2017; Che et al., 2019a). As the reaction carried on (Fig. S7b-d), more details on the TC degradation can be acquired from the variation in fluorescence intensity. As revealed in Fig. S7b, two new fluorescence peaks appear at λex/λem = (350–375 nm)/(420–450 nm) and λex/λem = (220–240 nm)/(400–450 nm), which may be due to humic acids-like and fulvic acids-like produced in the degradation process. In addition, the fluorescence signals increase with the reaction time in Fig. S7c, which means more TC molecules are degraded during the process. Extending the irradiation time to 90 min (Fig. S7d), the fluorescence intensity decreases to some extent, suggesting that the TC molecules had been degraded, as well as the humic acids-like and fulvic acids-like molecules can further be decomposed.

To further investigate the pathways during the degradation course of TC, the HPLC-MS/MS analysis was performed. Fig. S8 presents the HPLC-MS/MS spectra of TC at the degradation reaction of 0 min and 60 min, respectively. An intense prominent anion peak can be clearly observed at m/z = 445, which is coincide with the deprotonated TC (Deng et al., 2018). According to the detected intermediate products (marked by the number from A to G), the possible degradation pathways of TC are proposed in Fig. 5 . First TC with m/z = 445 is oxidized by hydroxyl radicals (OH) to produce compound A (m/z = 461) and B (m/z = 481). Subsequently, the N-methyl, amino, and carbonyl group on B (m/z = 481) are lost, leading to the formation of compound C (m/z = 453), D (m/z = 437) and E (m/z = 409), respectively (Li et al., 2018a; Tang et al., 2018; Che et al., 2018c). With the gradual detachment of branched chain groups, compound F with m/z = 347 is produced. Undergoing sequential redox reaction, the six-membered ring structure is opened to get compound E (m/z = 242) (Li et al., 2018b). Finally, the intermediate products may be degraded to smaller inorganic molecular materials and further degraded into CO2, H2O.

To investigate the charge carrier's separation and migration abilities of the as-prepared catalysts, which were monitored by transient photocurrents and EIS analysis (Che et al., 2019b; Li et al., 2017). Notably, as shown in Fig. S9a, the CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites display a much stronger photocurrent response than that of pure ZnIn2S4, BiOCl, and 10% ZIS/BiOCl composites. The result reveals that the CQDs not only provide reaction sites but also act as efficient electron acceptors to accelerate the separation of the photogenerated charge carriers, agreeing well with an observation that the degradation TC rate over the CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites is much higher than that of pure ZnIn2S4, BiOCl and 10% ZIS/BiOCl composites. In addition, the photogenerated charge carrier separation and transfer behavior for the samples were further investigated using EIS analysis as shown in Fig. S9b. Distinctly, in comparison to bare ZnIn2S4, BiOCl, and ZnIn2S4/BiOCl composites, the CQDs modified ZIS/BiOCl composites also shows the minimum radius of the arc. The experiments result implies that the 2D-2D VDW heterostructures and the introduction of the CQDs can accelerate interfacial charge transfer in the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites, thus effectively improving the photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and possible photocatalytic reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the recyclability and stability of the as-prepared catalysts, the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites of the TC degradation were reused four times under the same reaction conditions. In Fig. 6 a, the photocatalytic performance of the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl shows hardly any obvious changes, illustrating that the CQDs-ZIS/BiOCl composites possess superior photocatalytic recyclability. Simultaneously, the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of TC degradation over the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites was investigated in depth using the ESR and the trapping experiments. As shown in Fig. 6b, the degradation rate strikingly decreases when the ascorbic acid (VC, O2 − radical scavenger) was put into the reaction solution, suggesting that O2 − is the most reactive species in the degradation process. Meanwhile, the photocatalytic performance of the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites in degradation of TC is slightly reduced with the addition of EDTA-2Na (h+, radical scavenger) and isopropanol (OH radical scavenger), indicating that h+ and OH also contribute to the degradation of TC during the photocatalytic reactions. More importantly, to further confirm the existence of O2 − and OH radical species, the ESR technique was performed on 1 wt% CQDs-10% composites (Fig. 6c and d). The apparent characteristic peaks of the DMPO-OH and DMPO-O2 − are detected, further explaining that OH and O2 − are generated in the photocatalytic reaction system, respectively.

Based on the results of the above experimental analysis, the possible photocatalytic mechanism is proposed over the CQDs modified 2D-2D ZIS/BiOCl VDW heterostructure. As shown in Fig. 7 , when the 2D layered ZnIn2S4 is exposed to visible light and produce electron-hole pairs, then the electrons are excited from the valence band to the conduction band. Normally, the photoinduced electrons and the holes are easily recombined, leading to low photocatalytic activity over the single ZnIn2S4. However, when the CQDs and 2D BiOCl nanosheets are applied to the surfaces of 2D layered ZnIn2S4, the suitable band position and the strong VDW forces are conducive to a separation of the charge carriers. Therefore, the electrons in 2D layered ZnIn2S4 can be easily transferred to the CQDs, and the holes would migrate to the valence band of the 2D BiOCl nanosheets, which can improve separation efficiency of the electron-hole pairs and the enhancement of photocatalytic activity. In effect, the electron in the conduction band of the 2D layered ZnIn2S4 transfers to the surface CQDs and reacts with O2 to form O2 −. In addition, the holes cannot oxidize the H2O to generate OH because the valence band potential of 2D layered ZnIn2S4 is lower than the standard redox potential of OH (2.3 eV) (Zhang et al., 2018). However, the ESR and trapping experiments of active species all prove the existence of OH active species in the reaction process, which may be produced by further reduction of O2 − (Shi et al., 2016), as follows (Eqs (2)–(4)):
(2)
·O2 −+e−+2H+→H2O2
(3)
·O2 −+H2O2→·OH + OH−+O2
(4) H2O2+e−→·OH + OH−

Ultimately, O2 −, OH, and holes play a crucial part in the photocatalytic degradation process. Significantly, the visible light is emitted by the CQDs under NIR light irradiation, which is due to the UC-PL effect and can enhance solar energy utilization.

In summary, CQDs modified 2D-2D ZnIn2S4/BiOCl VDW heterostructures are first prepared by the hydrothermal method, which shows outstanding photocatalytic activity relative to pure BiOCl and ZnIn2S4 for degrading TC in water. The degradation rate constant of TC over 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composite reach up to about 42.5, 2.75 and 1.94 times than those of pure BiOCl, ZnIn2S4 and 10% ZIS/BiOCl composite, respectively. Meanwhile, the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites also show universality for degrading broad-spectrum antibiotics (TC, CIP, and OTC). More importantly, the degradation rate of TC of the 1 wt% CQDs-10% ZIS/BiOCl composites can reach up to approximately 17.7%, which is 9.31 times higher than the 10% ZIS/BiOCl composites under NIR light irradiation in 180 min. The improvement of photocatalytic activity is mainly attributed to the high charge carrier mobility of the VDW forces between 2D BiOCl nanosheets and 2D layered ZnIn2S4. Simultaneously, CQDs possess UC-PL effect as well as act as electron acceptors, which can enlarge the absorption range of light and suppress the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In addition, the degradation process and the decomposition pathways are proposed based on the 3D EEMs and LC/MS-MS results. Active species trapping experiments and ESR characteristic peaks reveal that O2 −, OH, and holes play a crucial part in the photocatalytic degradation process. Finally, this work may provide a design route to the quantum dot modification 2D-2D VDW heterostructures in its application for solar energy conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 51879228), the National Science Funds for Creative Research Groups of China (Grant 51421006), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2018B43614), the Program for Scientific Research Innovation Team in Colleges and Universities of Tibet Autonomous Region, and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2019.04.038.
